steps.dialogue: Before we finish this lesson, think about everything you figured out today! You worked through unit fractions like [fraction numerator=1, denominator=2]one half[/fraction], [fraction numerator=1, denominator=3]one third[/fraction][event:6910d570ba99ef23], [fraction numerator=1, denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction], [fraction numerator=1, denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction], and [fraction numerator=1, denominator=8] one eighth[/fraction]. You mastered non-unit fractions like [event:6910dab9b7d58da8] [fraction numerator=2, denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction], [fraction numerator=3, denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=5, denominator=6]five sixths[/fraction], and [fraction numerator=7, denominator=8]seven eighths[/fraction]. You even discovered that [event:6910dc0de996b3ea] [fraction numerator=1, denominator=2]one half[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2, denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=3, denominator=6]three sixths[/fraction], and [fraction numerator=4, denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction] are different names for the same position!

And guess what else? You didn't just place fractions; you developed the methods that work for you every time: things [event:6910de05931104eb] like equal spacing, [event:6910e0afdfc1afd9]careful counting, and accurate [event:6910df06fd13e6a5] labeling. You're growing into someone with strong math skills who really understands how numbers work.
You've learned three important things: fractions have exact positions, spacing must be equal, and patterns continue beyond whole numbers. With these skills, you can now place ANY fraction accurately!
In the next lesson we'll continue to build on what you've learned today to grow your math skills and explore even more fraction relationships.
